metric_value: Senior management consensus: productivity, customer satisfaction, revenue enhancement from effective IS strategy; 3-Tier architecture enables this
notes: Both public and private sector executives aligned on 3-Tier as strategic architecture

metric_value: Automate business information flows, improve productivity, add revenue opportunities, cut costs; replace batch mainframe with real-time processes
notes: First of four major customer requirements Aberdeen identified as benchmarks for 3-Tier success

metric_value: Flexible data access from multiple databases; data warehousing; real-time information access across enterprise
notes: Second requirement: distributed computing enabling access to multiple data sources

metric_name: 3-Tier business requirement 3: Scalability for emerging technologies
metric_value: Graceful evolution to new technologies; investment protection; SMP clustering and board-level upgrades increasingly popular
notes: Third requirement: SMP scalability as architectural workhorse

metric_value: Lower cost hardware (PC/LAN/mid-tier); reuse of existing mainframe applications; API bridges to legacy data; rapid application prototyping
notes: Fourth requirement: ROI without abandoning current systems

metric_value: Traditional 3-Tier + MPP/clustered SMP as fourth-tier for data warehousing and complex query; the 'Plus' in Three-Tier-Plus
notes: Aberdeen's original contribution: extending 3-Tier model to include MPP/data warehouse tier

metric_value: NT-based systems will begin to close sales gap vs Unix for mid-tier application servers within 24 months; will at least equal unit sales in workgroup
notes: Aberdeen notes Unix still superior at 4-8 way SMP in early 1996; expects NT to close gap by 1998

metric_name: NT Server mid-tier competitiveness actual outcome
metric_value: Windows NT Server 4.0 (1996) and NT Server 4.0 EE (1997) closed gap with Unix; NT became dominant mid-tier OS for enterprise application servers by 1998-2000
notes: Aberdeen's 24-month timeline prediction proved accurate; NT dominated enterprise by 1999

metric_value: NT Server beginning to dominate new LAN server shipments over Novell NetWare
notes: Aberdeen observes NT already winning LAN market before closing mid-tier gap

metric_value: As Oracle/Informix port to MPP and prices decline, MPP machines will achieve broader market acceptance as enterprise-class servers
notes: Aberdeen predicts MPP commoditization and mainstream enterprise adoption

metric_value: MPP data warehouse systems (Teradata, Netezza, later Amazon Redshift) became mainstream for enterprise analytics by 2000-2005
notes: Aberdeen's prediction confirmed; MPP/columnar DW became dominant BI infrastructure by mid-2000s

metric_value: Object-oriented programming techniques are critical to 3-Tier: enable application partitioning and deployment across tiers
source_page: Section IV: Relevance of Object Technology
notes: Aberdeen's core OOP thesis: objects map naturally to n-tier partitioned components

metric_value: Approximately 25 million business client workstations shipped worldwide in 1995
source_page: Section III: PC and Workstation Clients
notes: Quantitative market size anchoring PC LAN as dominant first-tier platform

metric_value: Residual value of mainframe software portfolio will continue to decline during second half of 1990s as PCs handle more processing
notes: Aberdeen predicts but notes mainframes will remain critical for high-volume transaction processing for 5 years

metric_value: Mainframe installed base shrank but did not disappear; IBM z-series still significant for banking/insurance; client-server/3-tier replaced most new development
notes: Aberdeen's nuanced prediction proved accurate: mainframes maintained critical role but 3-tier dominated new development

metric_value: Hardware, RDBMS platforms now stable, reliable, well-understood; missing pieces from 1992 (OOP tools, TP performance, data replication, network management) now in production
notes: Aberdeen notes 4-year maturation from 1992 whitepaper; sufficient evidence for IS executives to proceed

metric_value: OOP enables modular application development; partitioned components can be maintained/modified with fewest staff resources and shortest time
source_page: Section IV: Relevance of Object Technology
notes: Aberdeen argues OOP is key enabler for maintainability and adaptability of 3-Tier applications

metric_name: Data warehousing as Internet third-tier enhancer
metric_value: Internet browsers will explode 3-Tier information access with additional functionality, ease of use, and network throughput
notes: Aberdeen anticipates web browser as new information access layer on top of 3-Tier data warehouse

metric_name: Unix vs NT SMP performance comparison
metric_value: Unix 4-to-8-way SMP configurations superior to NT Server for higher-volume transaction processing; NT closing gap
notes: Aberdeen's competitive benchmark: Unix has 10-year field testing advantage on mid-tier SMP

metric_name: NT vs Novell LAN market prediction
metric_value: NT Server with NetWare interconnect services beginning to dominate new LAN server shipments over Novell NetWare
notes: Aberdeen assesses NT/NetWare coexistence strategy as NT displacement mechanism

metric_name: NT vs Novell LAN market actual outcome
metric_value: Microsoft Windows 2000 Server completely displaced Novell NetWare as dominant LAN OS by 2000; Novell market share collapsed from >70% to <10%
notes: Aberdeen's directional prediction understated the speed and completeness of Novell's displacement

metric_name: Aberdeen verdict: sufficient evidence to proceed with 3-Tier
metric_value: We have sufficient evidence for IS executives to proceed with confidence. Technology direction is clearer now. Products are more powerful and less expensive than ever.
notes: Aberdeen's definitive endorsement; marks 3-Tier as mainstream from emerging technology

metric_value: Object technology and application partitioning will be the method for maintaining/modifying modular development environments with fewest resources
source_page: Section IV: Relevance of Object Technology
notes: Aberdeen predicts OOP as mainstream for enterprise 3-Tier development

metric_value: OOP became dominant paradigm for enterprise application development by 2000; Java/.NET/C++ standard; prediction fully confirmed
notes: Aberdeen's prediction proved entirely correct; OOP universally adopted for 3-Tier and n-tier systems
